title: Architecture Patterns with Python
subtitle: Enabling Test-Driven Development, Domain-Driven Design, and Event-Driven Microservices
author: Harry Percival & Bob Gregory
description: A pragmatic guide to mastering architectural patterns in Python for building maintainable, testable, and scalable applications. This book systematically introduces Test-Driven Development, Domain-Driven Design, and event-driven architectures through a single example application built incrementally across chapters. It adapts proven enterprise architecture patterns such as Repository, Unit of Work, Message Bus, and CQRS for idiomatic Python, combining theory with working code.
keyIdeas: Repository pattern decouples persistence from domain logic, enabling testable data access
Unit of Work pattern coordinates transactional writes across multiple repositories
Aggregates enforce consistency boundaries within the domain model
Ports and Adapters (Hexagonal Architecture) isolates core logic from external infrastructure
Domain events capture meaningful state changes that other components can react to
Message Bus decouples command and event handlers for extensible workflows
CQRS separates read models from write models to optimize each independently
TDD drives architectural decisions by making dependencies explicit at test time
keyTakeaways: Start with the domain model first, then layer infrastructure around it — not the other way around
The Repository and Unit of Work patterns let you test business logic without hitting a database
Aggregates define transactional boundaries; keep them small and consistent
Dependency inversion (Ports and Adapters) makes your core framework-independent
Domain events let you model side effects as first-class citizens of the domain
A message bus decouples command handlers from event handlers, making the system extensible
CQRS is only worth it when read and write shapes genuinely diverge
TDD is not just a testing strategy — it is an architectural forcing function
whoShouldRead: Intermediate Python developers building complex applications who want to move beyond flat Django or Flask apps
Engineers transitioning from monolithic to event-driven or microservice architectures
Technical leads and architects seeking practical, code-first guidance on DDD and hexagonal architecture
Developers who know design patterns from Java/C# and want to apply them idiomatically in Python
